test_text.title: Information about Joining North College Library

audioscript.text: Section 1, you will hear a man telephoning a library to find out about joining.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example which has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning, North College Library.
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: How can I help you?
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: I was wondering if it was possible to join the library.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Are you a student at North College?
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: No, I'm not, but someone told me it was possible to join, even if I wasn't.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That's right, it is.
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: Are you over eighteen?
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: That's our minimum joining age.
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: Yes, I am.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: The minimum joining age is 18, so 18 years has been written in the space.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully, and answer questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning, North College Library.
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: How can I help you?
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: I was wondering if it was possible to join the library.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Are you a student at North College?
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: No, I'm not, but someone told me it was possible to join, even if I wasn't.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That's right, it is.
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: Are you over 18?
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: That's our minimum joining age.
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: Yes, I am.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That's no problem then.
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: Could you tell me what I have to do to join?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Well, you'll need to come into the library, and fill out some forms.
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: You'll also need to bring 2 passport photos with you.
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: We also need 2 documents for ID, so a driving license would be fine.
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: I've got that and what else?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Uh. Credit card?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: No, it needs to have your address on it.
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: Shall I bring a bank statement?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Would that do?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That'll be fine.
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: Good.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Does it cost anything to join?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Well, it's free for students here, but otherwise it's £125 per year, or £25 if you've got a current student card from another college.
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: I was at Westerly College until last year, but now I've got a job at Jefferson's steel factory.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Uh. It's more expensive than I thought.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: My local library is free.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: But you'll find they don't have the range of reference books or facilities which we buy for our students.
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: That's why you have to pay to be an external member.
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: I see.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Um. How many books can I borrow?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: We allow 12 items borrowed at any one time if you're a student, and that includes CDs, DVDs and videos.
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: However, it's only 8 items for members of the public.
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: Fine.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And how long can I have them for?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Well, you can have both fiction and reference books for 4 weeks, which isn't bad really.
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: And what happens if I return them late?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Like all libraries, there's a fine system in place.
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: The minimum fine is £1.50, but it can be much higher for some items, up to £5 per week.
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: We'll give you a booklet with all the details when you join.
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: You can always renew items if they're not required by anyone else, by telephoning or logging onto our website.
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the telephone call, you have some time to look at questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: What about the computers?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Can I use them free of charge?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: For college students, it's free, but for external members like yourself, the first hour is free, and then we make a nominal charge of £1 per hour thereafter.
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: Do I have to book in advance for them?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh, yes, it's advisable.
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: Most people tend to book 24 hours in advance, although sometimes you can get one with only six hours notice.
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: However, the earliest you can book a computer is 48 hours before you need it, and you can only book one hour at a time. If no-one else has booked the computer out, then you may be able to have another hour if you want.
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: We have a wide range of databases, so the computers are in great demand.
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: I'm thinking of doing some writing, and I might need to access national newspapers.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Do you have them on these databases?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: We do indeed.
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: We've got all the big nationals, The Guardian and The Observer, The Independent,
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: and The Times and Sunday Times.
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: We've also got all the local papers, and a wide selection of magazines.
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: Excellent.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Um. I assume you have photocopying facilities.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Of course. 5p (5 pence) a sheet for both A4 and A3 black-and-white copies, and 40p (40 pence) a sheet for color.
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: You can get a card from the counter here, it doesn't take coins.
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh, by the way, another thing I was wondering about was if you ran any writing classes through the library.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: We do, but you'll have to speak to John Grantingham about that.
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: He's our resident author, he runs the creative writing classes.
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: John Grant, uh, could you spell that for me, please?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Certainly.
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: GRANTINGHAM.
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: Are the classes here at the library?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, he's here on Thursday evenings.
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: Oh.
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: No, sorry, Friday, he's just changed it.
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: You can contact him by emailing the library.
audioscript.speaker: LIBRARIAN

audioscript.text: OK, right.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Well, that's about all I need to know.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Thank you.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I'll be along later this week to join.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Thanks.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Bye.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

test_text.title: Pedal Power: Bicycle Charity's Work and Appeal for Support

audioscript.text: Section 2, you will hear a man called Dan Pearman talking on the radio about Pedal Power, a UK charity which sends bicycles to people in developing countries.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 15.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 15.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: My name is Dan Pearman, and I'd like to talk about the work of Pedal Power, a small charity based mainly in the UK.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: I'll be giving our contact details at the end, if anyone would like to find out more about how to support us.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: But first, how the charity began.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: I got the idea of exporting bicycles to developing countries while I was in Ecuador.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: I went there in 1993 just after graduating from university.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: After three years of studying, I wanted adventure.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: I love traveling, so I decided to join a voluntary organization, and was sent to Ecuador to carry out land surveys.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: The project came to an end after five years, and when I returned to the UK in 1998 I started planning Pedal Power.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: Where I lived in Ecuador was a very rural area.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: My neighbor had the only bicycle in the village, whereas everyone else walked everywhere.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: My neighbor's business was unusually successful.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: And for years, I couldn't understand why.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: Then I realized having a bike meant he could get where he wanted to go without much trouble.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: Other local carpenters could only accept jobs in a 3 kilometer radius.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: So no matter how skilled they were, they could never do as many jobs as my neighbor.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: At Pedal Power, we collect second-hand bikes in the UK, and send them to some of the poorest regions in the world.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: When we distribute bikes overseas, we don't give them away for free.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: We'd like to, but long term that doesn't really help the local economy.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: The demand for bikes is enormous.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: Which makes them very expensive locally, so we sell them for 5% of the normal price.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: But in order to continue operating, we need to have a constant supply of bikes which we send out every six months.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: One example of a town that's received bicycles from Pedal Power is Rivas.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: It was the first place I sent a full container of bicycles to.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: Most people there now own a bicycle.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: The local economy has developed so much, you wouldn't recognize it as the same place.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: In fact, there are more bikes than on the streets of Amsterdam, if you've ever been there.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: Pedal Power still needs your help.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: You may have read about some of our recent problems in the British media.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: In August 2000 we simply ran out of money.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: We had containers of bikes ready to send, but no money to pay the bills.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: It was a terrible situation.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: We managed to ensure the bikes went out on time.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: But the other problems carried on for several months.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 16 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 16 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Fortunately in October 2001, we won an Enterprise Award, which helped us enormously.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: We invested fifteen of the seventy-five-thousand-pound prize money.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: To help secure our future.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: Winning the award helped raise our profile, and the money enabled us to pay all our shipping costs, which represent our greatest expense.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: Pedal Power changes lives.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: When someone gets a bicycle from us, they see a 14% increase in their income.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: We're currently looking to invest in computers so that our office staff can do an even better job.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: Because of our work, people in a number of countries now have a better standard of living.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: So far, we've provided 46,000 people with bikes, but would like to send more.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: At least 50,000 by the end of the year.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: Now, there are many ways in which you can support the work of Pedal Power, not just by taking a bike to a collection in your area.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: I should also like to say, if you do have a bike to donate.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: It doesn't matter what condition it's in.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: If we can't repair it, we'll strip it down for spare parts.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: Of course, to do that, we always need tools which are expensive to buy.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: So we welcome any that you can give.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: Also, you could help by contacting the voluntary staff at our offices.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: They'll be able to suggest activities you could organize to bring in funds for us.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: People do all kinds of things, including of course sponsored bike rides.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: Also, we're always interested to hear of other places that would benefit from receiving a consignment of bikes, and welcome suggestions from people who've been to developing regions on their travels.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: We hope that by talking on radio programs like this, we will be able to raise public awareness, which will lead to government organizations also giving us regular financial support.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: Something that we really need.
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: If you'd like some more information about where to donate an old bicycle or offer help in other ways, please contact us on...
audioscript.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Section 2, you will hear a man called Dan Pearman talking on the radio about Pedal Power, a UK charity which sends bicycles to people in developing countries.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 15.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 15.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: My name is Dan Pearman, and I'd like to talk about the work of Pedal Power, a small charity based mainly in the UK.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: I'll be giving our contact details at the end, if anyone would like to find out more about how to support us.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: But first, how the charity began.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: I got the idea of exporting bicycles to developing countries while I was in Ecuador.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: I went there in 1993 just after graduating from university.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: After three years of studying, I wanted adventure.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: I love traveling, so I decided to join a voluntary organization, and was sent to Ecuador to carry out land surveys.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: The project came to an end after five years, and when I returned to the UK in 1998 I started planning Pedal Power.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: Where I lived in Ecuador was a very rural area.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: My neighbor had the only bicycle in the village, whereas everyone else walked everywhere.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: My neighbor's business was unusually successful.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: And for years, I couldn't understand why.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: Then I realized having a bike meant he could get where he wanted to go without much trouble.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: Other local carpenters could only accept jobs in a 3 kilometer radius.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: So no matter how skilled they were, they could never do as many jobs as my neighbor.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: At Pedal Power, we collect second-hand bikes in the UK, and send them to some of the poorest regions in the world.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: When we distribute bikes overseas, we don't give them away for free.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: We'd like to, but long term that doesn't really help the local economy.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: The demand for bikes is enormous.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: Which makes them very expensive locally, so we sell them for 5% of the normal price.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: But in order to continue operating, we need to have a constant supply of bikes which we send out every six months.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: One example of a town that's received bicycles from Pedal Power is Rivas.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: It was the first place I sent a full container of bicycles to.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: Most people there now own a bicycle.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: The local economy has developed so much, you wouldn't recognize it as the same place.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: In fact, there are more bikes than on the streets of Amsterdam, if you've ever been there.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: Pedal Power still needs your help.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: You may have read about some of our recent problems in the British media.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: In August 2000 we simply ran out of money.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: We had containers of bikes ready to send, but no money to pay the bills.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: It was a terrible situation.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: We managed to ensure the bikes went out on time.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: But the other problems carried on for several months.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 16 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen and answer questions 16 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Fortunately in October 2001, we won an Enterprise Award, which helped us enormously.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: We invested fifteen of the seventy-five-thousand-pound prize money.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: To help secure our future.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: Winning the award helped raise our profile, and the money enabled us to pay all our shipping costs, which represent our greatest expense.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: Pedal Power changes lives.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: When someone gets a bicycle from us, they see a 14% increase in their income.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: We're currently looking to invest in computers so that our office staff can do an even better job.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: Because of our work, people in a number of countries now have a better standard of living.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: So far, we've provided 46,000 people with bikes, but would like to send more.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: At least 50,000 by the end of the year.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: Now, there are many ways in which you can support the work of Pedal Power, not just by taking a bike to a collection in your area.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: I should also like to say, if you do have a bike to donate.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: It doesn't matter what condition it's in.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: If we can't repair it, we'll strip it down for spare parts.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: Of course, to do that, we always need tools which are expensive to buy.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: So we welcome any that you can give.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: Also, you could help by contacting the voluntary staff at our offices.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: They'll be able to suggest activities you could organize to bring in funds for us.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: People do all kinds of things, including of course sponsored bike rides.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: Also, we're always interested to hear of other places that would benefit from receiving a consignment of bikes, and welcome suggestions from people who've been to developing regions on their travels.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: We hope that by talking on radio programs like this, we will be able to raise public awareness, which will lead to government organizations also giving us regular financial support.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: Something that we really need.
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: If you'd like some more information about where to donate an old bicycle or offer help in other ways, please contact us on...
utterances.speaker: DAN PEARMAN

utterances.text: That is the end of Section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: In 1993 Dan Pearman went to Ecuador
options: as a tourist guide.
as part of his studies.
as a voluntary worker.

items.title: Dan's neighbour was successful in business because he
options: employed carpenters from the area.
was the most skilled craftsman in the town.
found it easy to reach customers.

items.title: Dan says the charity relies on
options: getting enough bicycles to send regularly.
finding new areas which need the bicycles.
charging for the bicycles it sends abroad.

items.title: What does Dan say about the town of Rivas?
options: It has received the greatest number of bikes.
It has almost as many bikes as Amsterdam.
Its economy has been totally transformed.

items.title: What problem did the charity face in August 2000?
options: It couldn't meet its overheads.
It had to delay sending the bikes.
It was criticised in the British media.

desc.constraint: NO MORE THAN ONE WORD OR A NUMBER

items.title: How much money did the charity receive when it won an award?
What is the charity currently hoping to buy?

body.title: Which THREE things can the general public do to help the charity Pedal Power?
options: organise a bicycle collection
repair the donated bikes
donate their unwanted tools
do voluntary work in its office
hold an event to raise money
identify areas that need bikes
write to the government

test_text.title: A Feedback Session on a Student Video Project

audioscript.text: Section 3, you will hear a discussion between a college tutor and two students, Cristina and Ibrahim.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First of all, I'd just like to say to Cristina and Ibrahim that I really enjoyed watching your video about student life last week, and I could see that the rest of the group did too.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: You did really well, and I hope that you got a lot out of it.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: I'd like to use this tutorial as a feedback session where you reflect on the experience of doing the project.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: So, Cristina, I was wondering what you enjoyed most about making the video?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: I liked using the camera.
audioscript.speaker: CRISTINA

audioscript.text: Is it the first time you've operated one like that?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Yes, it is.
audioscript.speaker: CRISTINA

audioscript.text: Well, the results were very good.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Anything else?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: I also enjoyed visiting one of the British students we filmed.
audioscript.speaker: CRISTINA

audioscript.text: I'd never been inside a British home before.
audioscript.speaker: CRISTINA

audioscript.text: OK, Cristina, thanks.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Uh. What about you, Ibrahim?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: What did you enjoy?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Well, for me, it was a very good chance to get to know students who are on other courses, because everyone in our group is studying English, and we don't usually have much to do with the rest of the college.
audioscript.speaker: IBRAHIM

audioscript.text: Yes, good.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Do you think you'll maintain the contact now?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Well, I hope so.
audioscript.speaker: IBRAHIM

audioscript.text: I've invited 3 of them to have dinner with me next week.
audioscript.speaker: IBRAHIM

audioscript.text: Great.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: If you haven't decided what to make yet, I can tell you they'll love trying Arab dishes.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: And of course, it's good for your English too.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: And Cristina, what did you find?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: What was the most useful aspect of the project from the point of view of the English practice?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: I think when we were being shown how to edit the film, we had to follow the instructions, and that was very good practice for me, and I also learned some technical words that I hadn't heard before.
audioscript.speaker: CRISTINA

audioscript.text: What about you, Ibrahim?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: What was the most useful for your English?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: It was listening to the British students, because they don't speak as slowly as most of the tutors on our course.
audioscript.speaker: IBRAHIM

audioscript.text: I think they speak at natural speed, so it forces me to get used to it, and they use a lot of slang.
audioscript.speaker: IBRAHIM

audioscript.text: So you learned some new words which will be useful.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: IBRAHIM

audioscript.text: Good, I'm glad it helped.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Well, we've talked a little bit about enjoyment and about language practice.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Were there any other benefits?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: What else did you feel you'd learnt from the project?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Was it useful in other ways?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Yes, well, firstly I learned how to use a video camera.
audioscript.speaker: CRISTINA

audioscript.text: And also I think I really learned a lot about working together with other people.
audioscript.speaker: CRISTINA

audioscript.text: I've never done anything with a group before, and we had to find ways of cooperating, um, and compromising, and sometimes persuading people when they don't agree with you.
audioscript.speaker: CRISTINA

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: That is a very useful experience I know.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: What about you, Ibrahim?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Well, I think I learned a lot about how important editing is.
audioscript.speaker: IBRAHIM

audioscript.text: When you're filming, you think that everything is going to be interesting, but in fact we cut around half of it in the end, and then it was much better.
audioscript.speaker: IBRAHIM

audioscript.text: Good.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Well, one last thing I'd like to ask.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: What mistakes do you think you, as a group that is, made?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: I mean to put it another way, if you had to do it all over again, is there anything you'd do differently?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: We didn't plan very well, for example, we didn't decide on dates when we'd complete each separate step of the project.
audioscript.speaker: CRISTINA

audioscript.text: And we should have agreed about that in the beginning, because we were always late with everything.
audioscript.speaker: CRISTINA

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Anything else?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Hmm. I think we should have tried to experiment more with the camera.
audioscript.speaker: CRISTINA

audioscript.text: I mean with angles and the focus and that kind of thing.
audioscript.speaker: CRISTINA

audioscript.text: So you should have been more ambitious.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Do you agree, Ibrahim?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Not really.
audioscript.speaker: IBRAHIM

audioscript.text: In fact, I think we were too ambitious.
audioscript.speaker: IBRAHIM

audioscript.text: We were inexperienced, and we didn't have a lot of time, and we tried to do too much, to make a long film.
audioscript.speaker: IBRAHIM

audioscript.text: Hmm. Next time I would make a shorter one, and try to get the quality better.
audioscript.speaker: IBRAHIM

audioscript.text: Well, that's very interesting.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Next semester we will be doing another video project - with a different content, of course - but you'll have an opportunity to put into practice what you've learned this time.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Hmm. Do you have any ideas about a different...
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

test_text.title: An Introduction to Antarctica: Facts, Life, and Research Opportunities

audioscript.text: Section 4, you will hear a talk about scientific research in the continent of Antarctica.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Tonight I'm going to talk to you about that remarkable continent, Antarctica.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Remote, hostile, and at present uninhabited on a permanent basis.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For early explorers, it was the ultimate survival contest.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For researchers like me, it remains a place of great intellectual challenge, while for the modern tourist, it's simply a wilderness of great beauty.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: First, some facts and figures.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Antarctica is a place of extremes, the highest, coldest and windiest continent, and over 58 times the size of the UK.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The ice cap contains almost 70% of the world's fresh water, and 90% of its ice.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But with very low snowfall, most of the continent technically falls unbelievably into the category of desert.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Huge icebergs break off the continent each year, while in winter, half the surrounding ocean freezes over, which means its size almost doubles.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Research and exploration has been going on in Antarctica for more than 200 years, and has involved scientists from many different countries who work together on research stations.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Here science and technical support have been integrated in a very cost-effective way.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Our Antarctic research program has several summers-only stations, and two all-year-round ones.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I was based on one of the all year round ones.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The research stations are really self-contained communities of about 20 people.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There's living and working space, a kitchen with a huge food store.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A small hospital and a well-equipped gym to ensure everyone keeps fit in their spare time.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The station generates its own electricity.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And communicates with the outside world using a satellite link.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Our station - Zero One - had some special features.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It wasn't built on land, but on an ice-shelf, hundreds of meters thick.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Supplies were brought to us on large sledges from a ship 15 km away at the ice edge.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Living in the Antarctic hasn't always been so comfortable.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Snow build-ups caused enormous problems for four previous stations on the same site.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Which were buried and finally crushed by the weight.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Fortunately no one was hurt, but these buildings became a huge challenge to architects.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Who finally came up with a remarkable solution.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The buildings are placed on platforms, which can be raised above the changing snow level on legs which are extendable.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Food is one of the most important aspects of survival in a polar climate.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: People living there need to obtain a lot more energy from their food, both to keep warm, and to undertake heavy physical work.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Maybe you know that an adult in the UK will probably need about 1,700 kilocalories a day on average.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Someone in Antarctica will need about 3,500 just over double.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This energy is provided by foods which are high in carbohydrate and fat.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Rations for field work present an additional problem.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They need to provide maximum energy, but they must also be compact and light for easy transport.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Special boxes are prepared, each containing enough food for one person for 20 days.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You may be familiar with coffee processed by freeze-drying.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Which preserves the quality of the food product while making a large saving in weight.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well, this type of presentation is ideal in our situation.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It wasn't available to earlier polar explorers, whose diet was commonly insufficient for their health.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I think that being at the cutting edge of science has a special appeal for everyone working in Antarctica, in whatever capacity.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As a marine biologist, my own research was fascinating, but it's perhaps climate change research that is the most crucial field of study.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Within this general field, surveying changes in the volume and stability of the ice cap is vital, since these may have profound effects on world sea levels.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And on ocean currents.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A second important area is monitoring the size of the hole in the ozone layer above Antarctica, since this is an indicator of global ultraviolet radiation levels.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Thirdly, bubbles in the ice sheet itself provide an index of pollution, because frozen inside them are samples of previous atmospheres over the past 500,000 years.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And these provide us with evidence for the effects of such human activities as agriculture and industry.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There are an increasing number of opportunities for young people to work for a period in Antarctica, not only as research assistants in projects like mine.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But also in a wide range of junior administrative and technical positions, including vacancies for mapmakers.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I hope that the insights I've provided will encourage you to take up these opportunities in this fascinating continent.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Section 4, you will hear a talk about scientific research in the continent of Antarctica.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Tonight I'm going to talk to you about that remarkable continent, Antarctica.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Remote, hostile, and at present uninhabited on a permanent basis.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For early explorers, it was the ultimate survival contest.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For researchers like me, it remains a place of great intellectual challenge, while for the modern tourist, it's simply a wilderness of great beauty.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER
